title: ‘The Queen’s Gambit,’ ‘Small Axe,’ and the Top Five Miniseries of 2020
format: Sean and Amanda put movies aside to talk The Queen's Gambit, Steve McQueen's Small Axe, and the best miniseries of 2020, then Aneesh Chaganty stops by to talk his new thriller Run.

segments.heading: Wonder Woman 1984 Jumps to HBO Max

films.title: Wonder Woman 1984
films.note: Announced for a same-day HBO Max and theatrical release on Christmas, the closest a mainstream blockbuster has come to a true day-and-date release.

films.title: Palm Springs
films.note: One of the movies redirected to streaming this year that still found an audience.

films.title: Trolls World Tour
films.note: As Sean puts it, this one “walked so Wonder Woman 1984 could run.”

films.title: An American Pickle
films.note: Another pandemic-era streaming redirect they mention in the same breath.

segments.heading: Mangrove and The Trial of the Chicago 7
segments.note: Steve McQueen's Small Axe vs. Aaron Sorkin's courtroom drama

films.title: Mangrove
films.note: The first Small Axe installment. Amanda calls it one of the five best movies of the year, despite airing as a “TV show” on Amazon and the BBC.

films.title: The Trial of the Chicago 7
films.note: Watched back-to-back with Mangrove by accident; both are courtroom dramas about activists on trial, but Sean argues Mangrove is the tougher, more purely cinematic film.

films.title: Run
films.note: His new Hulu thriller, deliberately built as the opposite of Searching: a stripped-down two-hander about a mother, her daughter, and one very big secret.

films.title: Searching
films.note: His breakout, shot entirely on computer screens. That's the reason he wanted to prove next he could make something with “no gimmick.”

films.title: Shadow of a Doubt
films.note: One of Sean's reference points for Run's Hitchcockian dread.

films.title: Rear Window
films.note: Another Hitchcock touchstone Sean raises.

films.title: Misery
films.note: Sits in Chaganty's Letterboxd top four. Run leans hard on its captor-in-the-house tension.

films.title: What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?
films.note: A visual reference Chaganty cites, one he found only after the script was already written.

films.title: Psycho
films.note: Named alongside Misery as a source for Run's Mrs. Bates-coded references.

films.title: Alien 3
films.note: David Fincher's fraught studio debut, brought up as a parallel for a young director working with an unfamiliar crew.

films.title: Mank
films.note: Mentioned in passing as the reason Fincher's Alien 3 years are back in conversation.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: Cited as a film that changed how audiences weigh a movie's “theatricality” after premiering at home.

films.title: Leave No Trace
films.note: Chaganty's answer to the show's closing question. He admires how much drama it conveys without dialogue.

films.title: Columbus
films.note: Chaganty compares Leave No Trace's quiet control to this film.

films.title: Dick Johnson Is Dead
films.note: His other recent favorite. He calls its vulnerability something he's “not yet capable of.”

films.title: Contagion
films.note: Chaganty's pet Letterboxd grievance. He thinks its 3.6 average badly undersells it.

What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?.title: What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?
